content.text: Standard Support

Azure Standard Support costs $100 USD per month per subscription and is the minimum recommended tier for any production workload. Its defining feature over the Developer plan is 24x7 technical support with a Severity A response time of under one hour — critical when a production outage cannot wait until the next business day.

attrs.alt: Standard Support: 24x7 queue with Severity A (<1 hr), B (<4 hrs), C (<8 hrs) response times

content.text: Response Times and Severity Levels
Standard Support covers all three severity levels, all available around the clock regardless of time zone or day of week.
Severity
Definition
Response Time
Hours
A — Critical
Complete loss of business function; production fully down with no workaround
< 1 hour
B — Moderate
Partial loss; significant performance degradation; workaround exists but is painful
< 4 hours
C — Minimal
General questions, guidance, development issues, non-urgent debugging
< 8 hours

Severity A cases require you to remain available by phone during the support session. Azure engineers will request real-time collaboration — screenshare, live diagnostic commands — to resolve the incident as fast as possible.
Opening a Severity A Case via the Azure Portal API
When production is down, every second counts. Pre-scripting the case creation call lets on-call engineers open a Severity A ticket in under 30 seconds without navigating the portal under stress.

import os, requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

SUBSCRIPTION_ID = os.environ["AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"]
ON_CALL_PHONE = os.environ.get("ON_CALL_PHONE", "+15555550100")
ON_CALL_EMAIL = os.environ.get("ON_CALL_EMAIL", "oncall@company.com")
API_VERSION = "2020-04-01"

# Azure App Service service ID
APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID = "0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00"
# Production outage classification GUID (enumerate via /problemClassifications)
PROD_OUTAGE_CLASS = "32d322a8-acae-202d-e9a9-7371dccf381b"

def open_severity_a_case(
    resource_id: str,
    impact_description: str,
    ticket_name: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Open a Severity A (critical) support case for a production outage.
    Caller must stay available by phone — Azure may call ON_CALL_PHONE.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{SUBSCRIPTION_ID}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        f"?api-version={API_VERSION}"
    )
    body = {
        "properties": {
            "title": f"[CRITICAL] Production outage — {resource_id.split('/')[-1]}",
            "description": impact_description,
            "severity": "critical",
            "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID}",
            "problemClassificationId": (
                f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID}"
                f"/problemClassifications/{PROD_OUTAGE_CLASS}"
            ),
            "contactDetails": {
                "firstName": "On-Call",
                "lastName": "Engineer",
                "primaryEmailAddress": ON_CALL_EMAIL,
                "phoneNumber": ON_CALL_PHONE,
                "preferredContactMethod": "phone",
                "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
                "country": "US",
                "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
            },
            "technicalTicketDetails": {
                "resourceId": resource_id,
            },
        }
    }
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body,
                        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()
    print(f"Severity A case opened: {data['name']}")
    print(f"Status: {data['properties']['status']}")
    print(f"Support ID: {data['properties'].get('supportTicketId', 'pending')}")
    return data

# Example invocation from an on-call runbook
open_severity_a_case(
    resource_id=(
        "/subscriptions/your-sub/resourceGroups/prod-rg"
        "/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/prod-api"
    ),
    impact_description=(
        "Production API returning HTTP 502 for 100% of requests since 03:45 UTC. "
        "All instances show 'worker process failed to start' in App Service logs. "
        "Last deployment: 02:30 UTC (rollback attempted, no change). "
        "Revenue impact: ~$2,000/minute."
    ),
    ticket_name="sev-a-prod-api-20260701-0345",
)
Monitoring Case Status and Setting Up Alerts
After opening a Severity A case, keep a terminal session polling status so engineers can track progress without switching context.

import time

def poll_ticket_until_resolved(
    subscription_id: str,
    ticket_name: str,
    poll_interval_seconds: int = 60,
) -> None:
    """Poll a support ticket and print status changes until it closes."""
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        f"?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    last_status = None

    while True:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=15)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        props = resp.json().get("properties", {})
        status = props.get("status", "unknown")
        engineer = props.get("supportEngineer", {}).get("emailAddress", "unassigned")

        if status != last_status:
            import datetime
            ts = datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime("%H:%M:%S UTC")
            print(f"[{ts}] Status: {status} | Engineer: {engineer}")
            last_status = status

        if status in ("closed", "cancelled"):
            print("Ticket resolved.")
            break

        time.sleep(poll_interval_seconds)

poll_ticket_until_resolved(SUBSCRIPTION_ID, "sev-a-prod-api-20260701-0345", poll_interval_seconds=30)
Quota Increase Requests Under Standard Support

One of the most frequent uses of Standard Support is quota increase requests — expanding limits on VM cores, public IP addresses, storage accounts, or Azure Kubernetes Service node counts. These are classified as Severity C but are prioritised because they block deployments.
Checking Current Quota Usage

# List VM core quotas by region and family
az vm list-usage --location eastus2 \
  --query "[?contains(name.localizedValue, 'Standard D')]
           .{name:name.localizedValue, used:currentValue, limit:limit}" \
  --output table

# List network quotas
az network list-usages --location eastus2 \
  --query "[].{name:name.localizedValue, used:currentValue, limit:limit}" \
  --output table

# List storage account quotas
az storage account list --query "length(@)" --output tsv
# (max 250 storage accounts per subscription by default)
Submitting a Quota Increase via REST
Automating Severity-Based On-Call Escalation

A production monitoring system should create and escalate support cases automatically rather than relying on an on-call engineer to manually open one at 3 AM. The following pattern integrates Azure Monitor alerts with an Azure Function that opens a Severity A case when a critical alert fires.

# Azure Function: HTTP trigger invoked by Azure Monitor action group webhook
import json, os, logging, requests
import azure.functions as func
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential

app = func.FunctionApp()

@app.route(route="open-support-case", methods=["POST"])
def open_support_case_trigger(req: func.HttpRequest) -> func.HttpResponse:
    """
    Invoked by Azure Monitor action group.
    Creates a Severity A support case when a critical alert fires.
    """
    try:
        payload = req.get_json()
    except ValueError:
        return func.HttpResponse("Invalid JSON", status_code=400)

    alert_name = payload.get("data", {}).get("essentials", {}).get("alertRule", "unknown")
    resource_id = payload.get("data", {}).get("essentials", {}).get("alertTargetIDs", [""])[0]
    fired_time = payload.get("data", {}).get("essentials", {}).get("firedDateTime", "")

    credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    sub_id = os.environ["AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"]

    import re, datetime
    safe_name = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9-]", "-", alert_name.lower())[:40]
    ticket_name = f"auto-{safe_name}-{datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M')}"

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        "?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    APP_SVC_ID = "0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00"
    OUTAGE_CLASS = "32d322a8-acae-202d-e9a9-7371dccf381b"
    body = {
        "properties": {
            "title": f"[AUTO] Critical alert: {alert_name}",
            "description": (
                f"Alert '{alert_name}' fired at {fired_time} on resource {resource_id}. "
                "Auto-generated by on-call automation."
            ),
            "severity": "critical",
            "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{APP_SVC_ID}",
            "problemClassificationId": (
                f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{APP_SVC_ID}"
                f"/problemClassifications/{OUTAGE_CLASS}"
            ),
            "contactDetails": {
                "firstName": "On-Call",
                "lastName": "Automation",
                "primaryEmailAddress": os.environ["ON_CALL_EMAIL"],
                "preferredContactMethod": "email",
                "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
                "country": "US",
                "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
            },
            "technicalTicketDetails": {"resourceId": resource_id},
        }
    }
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    if resp.status_code in (200, 201):
        logging.info(f"Support ticket created: {ticket_name}")
        return func.HttpResponse(json.dumps({"ticket": ticket_name}), status_code=200)
    else:
        logging.error(f"Failed to create ticket: {resp.status_code} {resp.text}")
        return func.HttpResponse("Failed to create ticket", status_code=502)
Cost Optimisation: Sharing Standard Across Subscriptions

Standard Support is per-subscription, which makes it expensive at scale. For organisations with many subscriptions, Microsoft offers the Unified Support plan (successor to Premier) where a single agreement covers all subscriptions. Until that threshold, the practical approach is to ensure only production subscriptions carry Standard support.
Subscription Type
Recommended Plan
Monthly Cost
Personal / learning
Basic
Free
Development / sandbox
Developer
Staging / pre-production
Developer
Production (low traffic)
Standard
Production (revenue-generating)
Standard minimum
Regulated / enterprise production
Professional Direct
Practical Checklist Before Going Live on Standard Support
Verify the subscription has Standard (not Basic or Developer) support — check via Help + Support > Support plans in the portal.
Pre-create severity-tier response scripts and store them in your runbook repository.
Set up Azure Monitor action groups with phone/SMS alerts for Severity A-worthy metric thresholds.
Register the on-call phone number in the support case contact details — Azure engineers call this number for Severity A.
Test the Severity C case creation API end-to-end in a staging subscription before relying on it in production.
Document your resource IDs, service IDs, and problem classification GUIDs in the runbook so engineers do not need to look them up during an outage.
Building a Production Incident Runbook

Standard Support is most effective when your team has a pre-defined runbook that activates the moment an alert fires. The goal is to have a support case open and Azure engineers engaged within the first five minutes of a Severity A incident — not after 30 minutes of internal diagnosis.
Incident Response Timeline
Minute
Action
Who
Alert fires; on-call paged via PagerDuty / Teams / SMS
Automated
On-call confirms incident scope; runs triage script
On-call engineer
Severity A support case opened via pre-scripted API call
On-call engineer
Azure support engineer contacts on-call; joint investigation
Azure + engineer
Escalation if Severity A SLA breached; request duty manager
On-call engineer
Triage Script: Collect Evidence Before Opening the Case

"""
Incident triage script — run immediately when production alert fires.
Collects resource state, recent deployments, and metric spikes.
Output saved to /tmp/incident-<timestamp>.json for attaching to support case.
"""
import datetime, json, subprocess, requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def run_az(cmd: str) -> str:
    result = subprocess.run(f"az {cmd}", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
    return result.stdout.strip() or result.stderr.strip()

def triage(subscription_id: str, resource_group: str, app_name: str) -> dict:
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    ts = datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat()

    evidence = {
        "collected_at_utc": ts,
        "subscription_id": subscription_id,
        "resource_group": resource_group,
        "app_name": app_name,
    }

    # 1. App state
    evidence["app_state"] = run_az(
        f"webapp show --name {app_name} --resource-group {resource_group} "
        f"--query '{{state:state, kind:kind, defaultHostName:defaultHostName}}' --output json"
    )

    # 2. Last 10 deployments
    evidence["recent_deployments"] = run_az(
        f"webapp deployment list --name {app_name} --resource-group {resource_group} "
        f"--query '[0:10].{{id:id, message:message, active:active, time:lastSuccessEndTime}}' --output json"
    )

    # 3. Live instance count
    evidence["instances"] = run_az(
        f"webapp show --name {app_name} --resource-group {resource_group} "
        f"--query 'siteConfig.numberOfWorkers' --output tsv"
    )

    # 4. Resource health
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/resourceGroups/{resource_group}/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/{app_name}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/availabilityStatuses/current"
        f"?api-version=2022-10-01"
    )
    health = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=10)
    if health.ok:
        evidence["resource_health"] = health.json().get("properties", {})

    # 5. Recent HTTP 5xx count (last 30 min)
    evidence["http_5xx_30min"] = run_az(
        f"monitor metrics list "
        f"--resource /subscriptions/{subscription_id}/resourceGroups/{resource_group}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/{app_name} "
        f"--metric Http5xx --aggregation Total --interval PT5M "
        f"--start-time {(datetime.datetime.utcnow() - datetime.timedelta(minutes=30)).strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')} "
        f"--end-time {datetime.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')} "
        f"--query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data' --output json"
    )

    out = f"/tmp/incident-{ts[:19].replace(':','-')}.json"
    with open(out, "w") as f:
        json.dump(evidence, f, indent=2)
    print(f"Evidence collected: {out}")
    return {"path": out, "data": evidence}

triage("your-sub", "prod-rg", "prod-api")
Managing Multiple Production Subscriptions

Organisations with separate production subscriptions per team or product line face a cost challenge: each needs Standard Support at $100/month. The following script audits all subscriptions under a management group and flags those without adequate support plans.
Handling Severity A SLA Breaches
If Azure does not respond to a Severity A case within one hour, you can request escalation. The steps below outline what to do and how to track breach time programmatically.

import datetime, requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def check_sla_breach(subscription_id: str, ticket_name: str) -> dict:
    """
    Return whether a Severity A ticket has exceeded the 1-hour response SLA.
    SLA timer starts at case creation time.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        "?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=15)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    props = resp.json().get("properties", {})

    created_str = props.get("createdDate", "")
    status = props.get("status", "")
    severity = props.get("severity", "")
    engineer_email = props.get("supportEngineer", {}).get("emailAddress")

    if not created_str:
        return {"error": "no created date"}

    created = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(created_str.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    now = datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc)
    elapsed_minutes = (now - created).total_seconds() / 60

    sla_minutes = 60 if severity == "critical" else (240 if severity == "moderate" else 480)
    breached = elapsed_minutes > sla_minutes and engineer_email is None

    return {
        "ticket": ticket_name,
        "severity": severity,
        "status": status,
        "elapsed_minutes": round(elapsed_minutes, 1),
        "sla_minutes": sla_minutes,
        "engineer_assigned": engineer_email is not None,
        "sla_breached": breached,
    }

result = check_sla_breach(SUBSCRIPTION_ID, "sev-a-prod-api-20260701-0345")
if result.get("sla_breached"):
    print(f"SLA BREACHED after {result['elapsed_minutes']} min — request escalation via portal duty manager.")
else:
    print(f"Elapsed: {result['elapsed_minutes']} min / {result['sla_minutes']} min SLA. Engineer assigned: {result['engineer_assigned']}")

To escalate a breached Severity A case: in the Azure portal, navigate to Help + Support > All support requests > your ticket > Request duty manager. This routes to a Microsoft duty manager who can re-assign or accelerate the case.
Common Pitfalls When Using Standard Support
Pitfall
Impact
Prevention
Opening Severity A for non-production outages
Misuse wastes Sev A slots; Azure may downgrade severity
Reserve Severity A for production with zero workaround
Providing insufficient context in the description
Back-and-forth adds hours to resolution
Include resource ID, error text, steps to reproduce, and what you've tried
No phone contact available for Severity A
Azure engineer cannot proceed — case stalls
Always provide a reachable phone number in the ticket contact details
Not pre-scripting case creation
Manual portal navigation under stress adds 10–15 minutes
Maintain and test open_severity_a_case.py in your on-call runbook
Assuming Standard covers all resource types
Some preview or sovereign resources have limited support
Check the Support FAQ for your specific service before go-live
Summary

Azure Standard Support at $100/month per subscription provides 24x7 technical support with Severity A (< 1 hour), B (< 4 hour), and C (< 8 hour) response times, making it the minimum plan suitable for any production workload.
Severity A cases cover complete production outages — engineers are reachable at any hour and expect phone collaboration.
Pre-script support case creation using the Microsoft.Support REST API to reduce time-to-case during incidents.
The Quota API (Microsoft.Quota) provides a faster self-service path for many quota increase types without a support case.
Integrate case creation into Azure Function-based on-call automation triggered by Azure Monitor action groups.
Apply Standard to production subscriptions only; use Developer for dev/staging to control costs across a large subscription estate.
